author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content: I have relevant problems, and I know some other people have these, because it's difficult to manage risk. If you enter with Tony/Jdub, it might be later than them because the video might lag behind. Thus stop loss is wider. Scaling out at HoD makes RR less. This might lead to losing the same amount on losing trades that you gain on winning trades (and no continuation on trailers). If you can't manage to risk each time the equal amount, which is hard because you don't always know the stop loss potential risk when you enter the trade, you might end up red.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Hi guys, this might be interesting to anyone struggling with RR like I do. I revised my trades (made during live sessions) of the last month, and here's the stats.

There were 10 losing trades (exit on SL, actually 9, but for one trade I couldn't remember the setup so I counted it as a loss in terms of this analysis), and 12 winning trades. 3 of the losses were on FOMC day, so they can actually be excluded as I shouldn't have taken them at all. But still.

RR of the winning trades: 
9,39
4,73
4,07
2,08
3,29
2,05
4,32
3,04
1,4
3,16
5,18
3,3
That's if I didn't close position until the initial stop loss was hit and just waited until the trend changes or the day ends, and took profit at maximum (which you never know for sure, of course).

So there was only one trade with RR less than 2.
I'm actually red this month due to uneven risk management and selling 30-50% of the position at HoD most of the times (I trade 4-6 cons).

So I made these calculations to understand what kind of result I would have now if I only sold at 2RR or 3RR.
Check the screenshot, it's all there. 
If you trade a small number of cons as I do you might come to the same conclusion as I did, that even closing the whole position at 3RR without taking partial scales at HoD/LoD might have been rather profitable.

Of course, that's a random month and these stats aren't really representative. But I personally want to try something like selling 4 cons out of 6 at 2RR, and the rest (2) at 3RR.

Hope this is helpful 🙌

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
I never moved my SL to BE, I just scaled out 30-50% at HoD/LoD and scaled out the rest at psychological numbers and final target without any trailers. This led me to playing most of the time at RR 1:1, if not worse. That's why I decided to check the actual potential RR of the trades I took.

As for the analysis, you can see above that of my 9 losing trades I could restore, only 1 could use scaling out at HoD/LoD, and the rest of losing trades never reached HoD/LoD. So, if of my 12 winning trades I scale all of them 50% at HoD/LoD, that's 50% loss of potential profit of 12 trades, compared to this one trade where kinda saved 1/2 of risk by scaling out at HoD/LoD.

Of my trades of this month, many times price was kinda consolidating right after the entry, so closing position at BE didn't make any sense most of the time

Like this trade on AAPL I took on August 19th. Most of the time it's like this. The price is indecisive and then it breaks up or down. If I cut the position at BE, considering the entry price, the RR just doesn't make sense.

author: BullBearXInsights
content:
I’ve been part of several trading groups, and every time I joined one, I ended up blowing my account. When I traded on my own, I managed to make some profits. After joining this group, I blew my account again, and I realized it was because of me. I lacked focus and started relying too much on others' decisions instead of my own. I forgot that my purpose for joining the group was to learn from their trading mindset—how they think and trade. Believe me, Tony and Jack are the best compared to other groups out there.

One key lesson I learned was to do my homework, compare my analysis with theirs, and make decisions based on my own playbook. I started trading with smaller sizes, learning how they form their opinions. Interestingly, while I’m now closely aligned with their views, I still make slightly different decisions when it comes to entries and exits. I think these differences stem from their experience and account size. I feel more confident now, but as we all know, consistent trading success requires years of experience—at least three years of learning, and yes, blowing up your account (LOL).

The biggest lesson I’ve learned is the importance of cutting losses. There’s no reason to hold on to a losing trade. It’s like when you miss your stop at a train station—you should get off at the next one instead of waiting until the last stop. I hope everyone learns from their own experiences and finds success in the near future.

author: Jdub
content:
Just caught back up with the chat.

I'd like to give my thought on some of the things said.

Taking signals is not a long term solution. I can promise you that if you solely rely on signals that you will lose in the long term and let me break it down. A great example of this is yesterday and today. Let's say you took the NVDA trade towards the downside yesterday. Most likely you would have sold on the first push towards the downside because you didn't understand the overall context of the trade. This was a higher timeframe thesis laid out in the pre market prep and we talked about this over the weekend as well. If the market is weak we will be look for NVDA towards the downside. And if you didn't understand this, you would have no conviction to hold for the move towards the downside even though there was a very good chance that it would head lower. 

And today for example lets say you missed the trade yesterday and now you got into NVDA this morning. I mentioned that its a completely different setup and that you have to keep a tight stop on this one as its more aggressive since its close to open. Once again if you didn't understand this it would be hard to capture. Price ended up rejecting off PML and reversing. When it came back up I stopped out quickly because I knew that price was most likely going to reverse. 

What I'm trying to say is that the problem with taking signals is that when you lose you end up taking a full R loss and sometimes even more since you will hold past your stop and when your in a winning trade you sell as soon as your in the green. You can't hold because you don't have conviction for yourself since your relying on someone else but yourself.

And that's just the reality of taking signals. That's why we teach to fish for yourselves. You think Neto became profitable by taking signals. He took some of the concepts that we teach and implemented it to his own trading, where he has his own system, strategy and edge in the markets. 

You saw my st

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Slippage refers to the difference between the expected price of a trade and the actual price at which the trade is executed. This typically happens with market orders, which are executed immediately at the best available price. Since prices can change rapidly, especially in volatile markets or with large orders, the price you get may be slightly worse (or occasionally better) than the price you expected. 

For example, if you place a market order to buy a stock at $100, but by the time your order is executed the price has risen to $101, you experience slippage of $1. Slippage can happen in both buying and selling, and it’s usually more common during times of high volatility or low liquidity.

author: crunchybrewski
content: Most of the tickers we trade in this room have enough volume (contracts switching hands) that the bid-ask spread is pretty tight.  For a lot of my 0 day contracts, the bid ask spread is a few cents and there is enough volume to fill my order before the price moves too far in an unfavorable direction. For instance if I buy a contract for 1$ with the bid at .99 and the ask at 1.01. I can buy and immediately sell the contract for a loss of 2 dollars even without anything else changing because I bought at the ask and sold at the bid. tickers with less volume will likely have much wider spreads and so you can lose money even if the trade didnt move or moved in your direction. Hopefully that adds context to Neto's explanation.

author: Lane 🌿
content: I traded on my phone when I was a welder at a plant (super fast paced and strenuous) and had some success. I used WeBull because the mobile app is very very good and you can actually get in and out pretty quick. I bought one of those kickstand cases for my phone and I would put it in my welding cabinet where no bosses or anything could see. I kept 1 earbud in and had my sound on and notifications set on my charts for certain levels. Like for example the 5m range established so I'd go ahead and put an alert for above/below those levels by 1 cent. When they broke I'd get alerted, and when they'd come back to retest I'd get another alert! So I'd run over real quick and take my gloves off and get in the trade. Then I'd set an alert 10-15 cents above my stop so I'd get alerted and could stop out if needed. I'd have alerts 5 cents lower than each profit target to give myself time to get to my phone for the pop so I could scale out. I did this for about a year solid. It can be stressful, but once you kind of figure out your little way to do it and whatnot it can be done! Hope this helps man.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Hey @everyone,

Just a quick heads-up on why trading options on FOMC days isn’t recommended. The market tends to be super volatile, with quick and unpredictable swings that can throw off even the best setups. Options can get especially tricky because premiums can spike or drop suddenly, making it hard to manage risk. It’s often better to wait until the market settles after the announcement, when you can trade with more clarity and less stress. 

Play it safe—there will always be better, more predictable days ahead!

author: Jdub
content:
Its not really about being upset because as a human you are going to have emotions. It's all about what you do with those emotions. Am I upset when I take a bigger loss than expected. Of course but its all about how you respond and what you do after the fact. 

Trading is all a game about probabilities. I had conviction coming into this morning on TSLA for a continuation higher and guess what it didn't work out. I wanted to only risk around $2000 on this trade and ended up losing over $4000. But what happened next, I didn't do anything irrational or went to do something dumb. What I did was wait for another opportunity to setup. 

We ended up getting in SPY/ES which was a solid setup and is still working out as I type this out. 

As for relying on trading as income I didn't do this for the longest time. When I started out and even when I was profitable I was still delivering packages on the side. I would next recommend someone to do this full time without some sort of income coming in. Having to "rely" on trading is going to make it very difficult no matter who you are.

author: Mike Y
content: I've been studying and reading my MM5 notes and a piece that I wrote from Jdub word for word (I write this so not only myself but for many to connect with) :  Higher Time Frame Thesis: If your so fixated on lower time frame 1 min chart and you always sell on pops and take profits, sure you might make $400-$500 on 1 min time frame, but when HTF trade comes you've always sold on its pop. So when you are in a HTF trade you can not hold for HTF move. You can't take higher profits because you always come in as a scalpers mentality and sell on pops.  If you focus on HTF you will be able to capture more of the move.  But since you have a 1" lower time frame trading mentality-you will never capture higher time frame PROFITS!     I hope this connects as I am totally guilty of this!  Granted if $400 or $500 is all you want, I get it, but we as traders are not here to be limited.  to be restricted, to be reckless of trade by fear.  We are here to Capture, to provide and broaden our discovery to trading.  We can do this and we got this!! Just give it time and be patient!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Thank you so much for your kind words and for recognizing the progress I’ve made it truly means a lot. The turning point for me was realizing that trading is all about psychological thinking. I had to learn to trust my knowledge and analysis, stay focused, and, most importantly, put emotions aside. Scaling up became much easier once I disciplined myself to stick to my trading plan, even though the setups stayed the same.
I’ve failed many times in the past because I didn’t stick to my plan and let emotions take over. It wasn’t until I committed to following the plan and staying disciplined that things started to change. Your mention of the Plateau of Latent Potentialreally hit home it’s such a powerful concept. There were so many moments when I felt like all my hard work wasn’t paying off, but eventually, everything started to click. Breaking through that phase took persistence and mindset shifts, and it felt amazing.
And no, I wasn’t upset at all by your gesture it actually made me happy! It’s great to know someone else understands and celebrates these milestones with me. Thank you so much for your support and encouragement it truly means a lot. 

Trust the process:)

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**__RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY__**

Listen up, because this is crucial. Take a screenshot of this and pin it somewhere visible.

**If you're in the green for the week**, only gamble with a tiny slice of your gains. Say you're up $1,000, then play with just $200-$300. Even if you blow it, you'll still walk away with a profit for the week.

If you're green but your profits are too slim to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you're in the red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

And even if you're sitting pretty with some profits to burn, consider taking Friday off. An extra day to relax isn't something to sneeze at.

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. __**

Follow these rules, and you'll never end the week wishing you hadn't played.

@everyone <@&1219020123218645102> 

Please confirm you've read this with a 🤑

author: Angel (az)
content:
I can't speak for the pros, but here is my personal experience as a beginner - I'm able to hold the trade and not care because I am comfortable with the risk im exposed to at 2 cons total, I am comfortable with it dropping 50% in value overnight in the worst case. And 2 is easier to manage than 1 IMHO. I don't know how you're sizing but it needs to be what you personally are comfortable with mentally at this point in your journey. I am strict with scaling out at the first pop up which gives you realised gains and it's like a cheat code that makes it easier for you psychologically to hold the second one. The Tesla trade this week for example, my first con scale was 300+ realised, so now psychologically, i am okay with my second con going to -300. (I would not wait that long to stop out, but it lets you breathe and gives you a lot of room mentally). Ever since then, I literally try to not even look at the value of my second con or the price, I am just waiting for the HTF target to hit or structure to break. 

If the trade doesn't work: I believe Jack or Tony said you have your stop losses as usual in terms of trades not going your way, but you have to be more comfortable with a larger percentage drawdown. Stop loss is at where key HTF levels and structures are broken

author: Angel (az)
content: Np 🙂 stay as long as the market dictates since we can't predict when or if it'll actually happen. You just take it day by day and stay in your trailer con until 1) HTF take-profit target hits or 2) structure breaks, and stop loss hits. Case 3 I imagine is expiration day hits and neither 1 nor 2 happened, in which case I would probably reevaluate whether everything still looks solid, and close this week's and decide whether to buy next week's expiration. (Have to check with others on this).

author: Angel (az)
content: Oh and in case 1, if there are no more HTF levels then you start monitoring the chart real time and have to use more discretion/experience when to close out your last trailers. I would probably try to make myself a rule that's more mechanical since I still lack that experience. So probably something like - I must take profit after first bearish candle closes after ATH, or, after first close under an order block after ATH. That way I have a clear exit plan while I hopefully eventually gain that experience

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content: Order blocks and liquidity zones look different on 1-minute and 5-minute charts because they show price movements over different time periods. The 1-minute chart shows more small, short-term activity (noise), while the 5-minute chart combines those movements into bigger, clearer zones where major buyers or sellers are active. Use the 1-minute for quick trades and the 5-minute for spotting bigger trends and stronger levels

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Validate Trendline: Ensure it connects two or more significant swing lows/highs without cutting through candles. Retests confirm validity.
Trendline Break: Redraw if price closes decisively beyond the trendline with strong momentum or volume.
New Structures: Adjust for new formations, like flags or consolidations, as they develop.
Confluence Levels: Prioritize trendlines that align with key support, resistance, or Fibonacci levels.
Timeframe Matters: Short-term trendlines (1-min, 5-min) may require frequent adjustments due to volatility.
Price Behavior: Watch for tight consolidation or "riding" along the trendline as a signal for potential breakout.
Stay Flexible: Update trendlines dynamically based on real-time market changes and dominant structures.

author: Nines
content: Np, another tip is you go long until a key level on the HTF is been met. Because you have to think of it like this, price is not going to magically spawn to they key level. It has to get there somehow and there are alot of A+ setups that form to get you to that key level

author: Nines
content: This is essentially how you counter trend. Say your in a bearish market and you want to take a short but the next bearish key level is a bit far away and you dont wanna short at the current price because its over sold. You wait for order flow to switch from bearish to bullish on the 1h and 4h and then you take a long until that key level is met. Its crucial that you let a bottom form first and not just try and catch the bottom. Never ever try and predict the bottom u let it form and then play the counter trend

author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
I took the put trade yesterday because of multiple confluences. Price had retraced to the 61% fib level which correlated closely to the KL from previous PDH (I read trading bible over last weekend in books channel), PA was showing wicks up for a few bars showing sellers keeping price from breaching, there was a perfect test of the 1min ORB low and I think at the same time Qs and Spy showed weakness. 
I did not expect it to go down that quickly and exited all cons in the same fashion missing out on the huge move downward. I failed to leave trailers and scale. Also exited completely because just minutes prior I was in a call that at peak was 3x what I closed scaling 25% then 75% at breakeven. One lesson is I need to remember to leave trailers. Missed the big move downward. could have been a $3k day instead of $550 if I left trailers. That said I am happy that I am starting to see profitable entries.

content: the 119 is 1 otm since stock price is 118. 1 otm strikes are a good balance for day traders to take. usually they have a good delta and the cost is decent. so it makes for good profit compared to cost. if you look at 2 otm sometimes the volume and open interest paired with your thesis on thinking the stock will go to $120 from 118 can be more profitable with a strong move towards the strike price. its really all about your risk and thesis on where the stock will go

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**__RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY__**

Listen up, because this is crucial. Take a screenshot of this and pin it somewhere visible.

**If you're in the green for the week**, only gamble with a tiny slice of your gains. Say you're up $1,000, then play with just $200-$300. Even if you blow it, you'll still walk away with a profit for the week.

If you're green but your profits are too slim to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you're in the red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

And even if you're sitting pretty with some profits to burn, consider taking Friday off. An extra day to relax isn't something to sneeze at.

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. __**

Follow these rules, and you'll never end the week wishing you hadn't played.

that’s the way to go bro. It took me months to figure out why I was losing so much money. I finally figured out it was actually 2 big reasons:

author: NickA
content: joined only 1 week ago... Copying them straight up has given me winners and losers.... but you have to handle your own risk/sizing/etc.  Try to make it about the process and evolving away from copying T and J to following along to learn the decision making and flow... easier said than done...  improve scanning technique on multiple symbols and id'ing the key levels with them....  study their pre-market strategy and real-time thought processes and adjustments... it's game changing for me...  seeing them in action helps me modulate my trading frequency and build a better eye for hitting better trades.   Still trying come up with my own actionable trades with better entry/exit plans every day and calming down in general.  Using their actions and comments as a "confluence" but not the only reason to jump in a trade is the pattern I am trying to break.... I am gaining more comfort with the idea of doing it solo. Still so much growth required (and accelerator training) but it's starting to slow down and feel more settled thanks to both T, J, and the community.

author: Nines
content:
some food for thought for you guys; 

scaling out or taking partials are for those who dont trade the bigger picture. reason u never see me scaling out a contract and holding to my tp because it skews my RR significantly. instead of making 6R ill make only 2.5R or lower. then you wonder why your losses are as big as your winners or even bigger. 

with option its tricker because you almost have to scale out because greeks will kill you if you dont, but if you trade futures or forex i recommend you hold to your tp because the days you lose which you will, it wont hurt because your constantly catching 5R trades on the regular. 

im not saying this to bash on anyone who scales out if that works for you and you have success doing that then keep doing it but for me in my experiene i used to see my losers were as big as my winners even tho i was catching 6R trades but when i would scale out at hod or a random level i had marked it skewed with my RR

author: Kam
content:
hey there, while the key levels to take a trade may be different - the qualifying criteria for a break & retest at any level is the same (e.g. clear displacement; strong/weak price action at level, etc). 

using the 1m opening range is usually a more aggressive entry model, so if using the 1m OR we ideally like to look for confluences at that level (orderblocks or an opening play like a gap & go scenario, pre-market level, news, etc). 

Because of the big gap-down today and aggressive continued selling at the open, using a more aggressive entry model on shorts was necessary to try and capture continuation since in most gap-down scenarios we like to look for pops into higher timeframe levels before looking for setups - this generally leads to a higher probability & longer duration trades on the short side.

the best way to get deeper on this to to backtest and log some data to figure out which levels work best for you and on which tickers

also check out Tony's trade review on NVDA today; he goes over why the 1m opening range setup nicely after the gap-down with orderblock & PML confluence

<#1219022089252503632> 

 hope that helps!

author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
Part of the risk with holding overnight and over weekend. A war could breakout, terrorist attack, natural disaster, fraud report, etc. 

Only trade with what you’re willing to lose because overnight is a crapshoot. 

Very unfortunate that Moody released the downgrade. I would even guess it was strategically timed as the market has rebounded from earlier lows. We just have to accept it as part of the game. 

Once you have a proven system to trade and confidence from back and forward testing, it is easier to accept the market for what it is, uncertain. 

Your edge in uncertainty is your back and forward tested system with proven probable edge and sound risk management. 

You have to know if we go to war overnight you’re okay with losing whatever is in the market overnight. 

I have lost quite a bit from following alerts and it has made me a better trader.

author: Brute7
content:
This was drawn manually before I created the script, and I hope it helps illustrate my experience.

Without R3 marked, many intraday traders might continue holding their positions, expecting further upside. Due to FOMO, they may end up selling only after the price starts dropping. Since I have R1, R2, and R3 plotted, I would sell a few contracts at R1 and the remaining at R2 and R3.

Again, at R1 we saw strong price action, which drove the move all the way to R3 and beyond. This allowed me to re-enter the trade at R1, sell a few contracts at R2, and the rest at R3.

author: Brute7
content:
<@739730143693766676> Great question! The S/R levels in this script are classic Floor Pivots, which are always calculated from just the previous regular session’s high, low, and close—not by looking for swing points or historical pivots “to the left.” This makes them uniformly spaced and mathematically derived, not price action-based.

This style is perfect for scalping and intraday trading, because it gives you instantly actionable levels that reflect the market structure from the most recent session—great for gaps, mean reversion, and fast trades at key S/R zones right from the open.

There’s a ton of room to customize

You could script additional pivots using longer historical data (multi-day, multi-week, or even price-action “look left” swing pivots).

You can also add as many custom levels or dynamic pivot algorithms as you want, depending on your strategy.

If you want a “look left” style (true swing or historical pivots), that’s a very different type of script—totally possible, but would need to be designed to scan X bars back for true support/resistance.

This one is focused on consistency, speed, and using “yesterday’s map” for today’s trades.

**However, I like your suggestion and I will release v4.0 version to plot the pivots and S/R levels from the last 10 sessions as well.**

author: Brute7
content:
<@1148249290293116990>:
The gap line and label in the script are specifically designed to show the difference between the previous day’s close and the current day’s 9:30am open.
It should not be referencing the previous day’s open; the true “gap” is defined as “current session’s open at 9:30am ET minus previous session’s close.”
If the gap label appears at the wrong price or isn’t updating correctly, it’s usually due to chart timing (e.g., before the new session starts) or a possible visualization glitch, especially on non-intraday timeframes.

After the new session opens at 9:30am ET, the gap label should appear at the current session’s open, showing the value “current open – previous close.”

Just published new version (v4.0) with ON/OFF toggles for all key levels!

What’s New in v4.0:

ON/OFF Switches for Every Level:
Easily toggle the display of each key level (Prev High, Prev Low, Prev Close, Pivot, R1, R2, R3, S1, S2, S3, and Gap) directly from the indicator’s settings panel.

Improved Usability:
Show only the lines and labels you care about—customize your workspace and reduce chart clutter instantly.

No Visual Duplicates:
All objects are managed cleanly: turning a level OFF fully hides its line/label, with no overlap or ghosting.

<@739730143693766676>:
I’m working on adding 10-day key levels to the same indicator script so you can plot historical key levels from the last 10 sessions.
Since this will significantly expand the code and potentially affect chart performance, I’ll publish it as a separate indicator.

author: GoodVibez
content: I consider it every single time. Tony and Jack often mention sizing for zero. It's why strength/momentum is very important. I will often buy an extra week out just to help account for some of the loss due to theta if I see something on HTF, but it doesn't move right away and chops around, but never hits my stop. Yes, buying further out requires a little more of your capitial and puts more of it at risk should the trade move against you w/ lots of strength. Why we have stops though right. But the plus side, is that if you have conviction in your HTF thesis and just feel it needs a little more time to play out, it gives you the ability to hold a trade longer and not have Theta eat as much liquiditiy as fast. This may be beneficial for someone who doesn't have a margin account, has limited funds, and is limited the amount of day trades they can enter w/n a week. They don't have the luxury of opening and closing as many trades.

author: Brute7
content:
It’s officially been one month since I started my intraday trading journey — and it all began with inspiration from the Scarface YouTube channel. What started as simple curiosity quickly turned into a daily commitment to grow, learn, and show up — regardless of profits or losses.

Like many beginners, I dove into breakout, retest, and price action strategies, thinking I had a solid handle on options trading. But over time, I realized there’s so much more to it — market news, economic reports, index correlations, relative strength, option Greeks, and key levels all play a critical role. One of my early mistakes was spreading my attention too thin, tracking too many tickers without focusing deeply on any.

So three days ago, I made a shift: I committed to just five tickers — SPX, NVDA, TSLA, QQQ, and SPY. Since then, I’ve seen a clear difference. Studying these names daily has helped me understand their behavior better, enter trades with more precision, and exit with more confidence.

Today, I hit my personal all-time high in trading. It’s not $10k (yet), but I know I’m on the right path — one day at a time.

author: Kam
content:
Hey Viper - this is definitely a loaded question lol.
Hard to answer without seeing more of your data or trading patterns, but here are a few thoughts that might help:

**Plan your levels + scenarios**
If you don’t have a mapped path for where 3R–5R could come from (HTF levels, gaps, reclaim zones), it’s hard to stay in the trade. Build the scenario before you enter.

**Revisit the HOD scale**
If you're trading a small number of cons and scaling at 1R/HOD, you're likely choking the trade by taking a large % off before the trade really gets moving. That said, don’t throw out the HOD rule blindly -  let structure & context guide 

**Mind your strike**
Pay attention to where underlying price is relative to your strike when you hit HOD. A lot of the juice in options comes from delta expansion past the strike - not just tagging it.

**Trailer logic **
Even something simple like bar-by-bar, 9EMA, or last HL break helps you stay in runners. Without a plan, it's easier to flatten early.

**Journaling & Expectations**
Which setups actually run 3-5R+? Even if you scaled out early, make sure you're tracking which trades run. Not all of them do. Also keep in mind that even if the max R is 5R, most likely you won't be able to capture the full 5R due to scaling into pops. I try to aim for 50-60% "R capture" as a metric I track regularly. 

Let me know if you want to walk through a specific trade. feel free throw one up in <#1219021113686888478>  and tag me - happy to dive in.

author: Hayden
content:
- Candle Close Stop Loss

Pros:

Filters out “wick hunts” and intraday noise.

Keeps you in trades that recover quickly after a fake-out.

Helps align with structure rather than random ticks.

Cons:

Risk can be larger than expected, since by the time the candle closes, the option premium may be down much more.

Hard to execute in options intraday because option premiums are extremely sensitive (gamma, delta shifts). A stock moving $0.30 against you can crush the premium more than expected.

Can lead to hesitating, hoping the candle “saves” the trade, which sometimes just means deeper loss.



- Hard Stop at Exact Price

Pros:

Clear, mechanical, no decision fatigue.

Protects capital better, especially in options where theta + gamma magnify losses quickly.

Removes emotional “what if it comes back?”

Cons:

You’ll get wicked out more often, especially intraday.

Slippage can hurt — a 10–20¢ slip in stock can translate into 20–30% option loss.

Misses some trades that recover immediately after the fake-out.



- Slippage Reality in Options

Options don’t move smoothly with the underlying; spreads + illiquidity + IV shifts = exits are rarely clean.

Market orders at stop get filled worse than stock.

Sometimes you get slipped on size even if spreads look tight.

This is why many intraday options traders actually prefer mental stops or alerts and exit manually, because hard stops can trigger at the worst possible premium.


Simplification: If you hesitate a lot on your stops, you have a small account, use exact stop levels. (Manual or Orders) The BNR usually works right away or it doesn't at all so most of the time (In my opinion) its worth it to close out. Tony and Jack have a lot higher risk tolerance than most and have been trading price action for many years.

If you wait for closures - accept the risk you're R/R might be skewed on a lot of your trades but your win % might be higher (if they reverse), better for experienced traders

author: Funkdamentalist
content:
I totally agree.... I guess it's human nature to want to put the blame somewhere... anywhere really.... except for where it should be placed... with yourself.  No one is pushing those buttons except for us.  Tony and Jack's modules, youtube videos, and even live trades state clearly the risk management side of things and how important that is.

With me, I have to see if the trade makes sense for ME before getting in... I didn't take the TSLA trade because to ME it hadn't setup... 

Jack showed the important levels to watch pre-maket and specifically said he wanted to see TSLA pull back and THEN break to the upside... well, TSLA never did that. Even though Jack and Tony got in, they said it was an aggressive move on their part... I'm not as skilled and don't have the capital they have so for me it wasn't even an option.

I looked for other opportunities... I think that's the whole point... we learn and apply the learnings to OUR situation and our risk profile and according to what our accounts allow us to trade. After that we need to analyze the trades, and look in the mirror and assess what we did wrong and if we have questions to ask for help here

author: Jdub
content:
This is not an alert service. 

If you want alerts this is not the place to be. 

What matters is the risk amount and thesis. 

If you just copy alerts it doesn't work because you don't understand the thesis and logic behind the trade. 

Even if you get the same entries ultimately if you don't build your own thesis and confidence you won't be able to hold onto the trade if it works out because your reliant on someone else besides your self.

author: Jerry
content:
Hey guys,

Wanted to share my experience of going from struggling to profitable (just a few months so far, but still). Maybe it can be a good starting point for some of you—it really changed the game for me.
I struggled a lot before. At one point, I was trading 20 TSLA contracts, taking small wins but then wiping everything out with big losses. Ended up down almost $15k in just a few months. That’s when I stopped and decided to really think.
I built an Excel table with all of Tony’s trades this year, split by symbol. Every row was a trade with entry (I added some delay since I’m slower), stop loss, max potential win until SL was hit, etc. I kept adding parameters and I’m still revisiting them, improving execution.
From that analysis, I found the main thing that killed my profitability: scaling out too early. I used to sell 30% on the HOD pop, then more on the next big candle, and my RR was completely ruined.
So I made a script in Excel to simulate different scenarios and find the best monthly outcome. The result was simple: don’t scale at all until TSLA moves at least $4 (around 2R), then exit. That’s it.
That one change completely flipped my trading. Now I trade 10 contracts and make way more than I did before with 20. This is just the first step, of course. Next is working on dynamic sizing based on setup quality, scaling in/out properly, etc. But this shift was the key—it stopped the bleeding and got me back to making money.

Hope it will help

author: Luke
content:
Since they're 0DTE contracts I play them a lot differently than I would futures contracts.
a) I'm still developing and refining my playbook, but what I usually do is trail it by the break of structure on the 1min after a decent move where the contracts are at least 15-20%. When there is a flush and the contracts start to move, market structure starts to really play out and be respected.
b) On these setups a lot of times when it consolidates during the trade and starts to stall and not move down, there can be really impulsive green candles which crush the contracts. They're usually easy to spot within the first few seconds of the candle because buyers aggressive start stepping in. In that case I'll usually stop out once candle just gets above my invalidation level. What I'm trying to work on right now is not letting green trades go red.
Hopefully that makes sense let me know if it does.

content:
hey brother I wish I could just tell you to keep the fight and be patient, but that will not be sincere from me. 
I would like to offer you two ideas to challenge your thinking. 

aa) Please review your stop loss rule and your adhering to it like your life depends on it. Forget about the noise from all of us. Considering yout modest wins,  If you lost $450 following Jack’s trading even  if he was wrong, there is someting substantially untold about your stop loss and you dragging it, or that your size went dramatically up in an attempt to recover previous losses.  Examine this. Controlling your risk and losses is our primary job. It is not winning unlike most people may think. 

bb) You have a point about the criteria of entries used by the coaches. I do not think is conservative enough. Just my opinion. The reason it works overtime for Jack and Tony, is that I think they have mastered their egos and risk control and they exit quick and according to their plan and ruthlessly about it.  No second thoughts on hope do they have. So their losses are small compared to their big wins. But you need to back test everything we say to you. 
This is what I an trying to improve on the effectiveness of my entries. I wait for a 5 min candle formation doing higher lows or lower highs in respect of the adverse candles color, and will enter only when the 5 min chart confirms to me so. I will journal my results and evaluate this approach after 50 trades starting tomorrow. I observed in backtesting this has a large rate of win. Some trades will just take off and you will not be able to enter them with this method.  But the market will be there tomorrow and you as well.

author: Luke
content:
With these trades I scale them purely based on the underlying key levels and not the contract's percentage. How I personally use the contract's percentage to my advantage so avoid unnecessary losses is once i'm in at least 15-20% ish profit (which if you get a move down right away will automatically happen) I don't want to make it a loss so I won't hold to my stop loss. The only time I will hold to my stop loss is if the trade doesn't work out right away. So like at my entry here you can see I wanted to scale 40% at 659, but it didn't hit it and rejected, and my contracts went to around breakeven. If there was any strength after that 10:17 candle I would've stopped out immediately with a super small loss (pretty much breakeven).
As far as the R:R tool goes on the underlying chart. With 0DTEs you can't really base it off that much because they move way differently. Sometimes on the underlying you'll be up 1:1R but the contracts are up almost 2R. One thing I personally will do is if the contracts move to 40% and i'm not at TP1 yet, i'll scale some no matter what because that's like a 2R on the contracts because on these type of trades you should be losing about 15-20% on your losses.
So for your first trade that was up 30%, if I hadn't hit TP 1 yet then I would for sure have stopped out when the contracts hit breakeven, because you can always look to re-enter, so just save yourself from a loss and reevaluate. For your second trade, if it hadn't hit TP 1 yet and the contracts were at 30%, then I would've stopped out breakeven. How I play it is when the contracts are at 40% (2R for the contracts) and price rejects, i'll sell the contracts at 15-20% in profit no matter what so that it doesn't become a red trade.
Those are just some rules i've made for myself over the past weeks of trading thse, hopefully that helps

author: EeveeWinter
content: Part of it is momentum. Tony and Jack always say “scale on pops! Or scale on strength!” That’s because the contracts are juiced in those moments and they provide a nicer market value. Same goes for when a trade reverses. Let’s say the upside is moving slowly and then all of a sudden some red candles form quickly to your stop loss. You’re now cutting your loss into weakness and you’ll feel it more. Today was a perfect example on that first NVDA trade. When Tony cut it, he had to take a bigger loss because that candle that broke structure was big and fast. Bigger loss.

author: healthiswealth
content: I had this same question as well. I was winning, but the losses were twice as big. What I noticed was that I was not scaling out my wins properly. Also, I didn't have trailers. Having trailers are HUGE in order to maximize a win. There have been trades where Tony and JDub have made more from the trailers than they did at the first and second scale outs. So, when we don't scale out properly and have trailers, losses will be greater than the wins because we don't match Tony/JDub's exact winning formula and we "leave meat on the bone", but we do match exactly the formula when they take a loss.

author: Markellwhite16
content: Absolutely! And for a small account tbh I wouldn’t really focus too much on runners . Every trade you take you should focus on high RR trades and let them play out. Becuse if your scale out you’re not going to make much with 1-2 cons you get me ? You’re better accepting the risk , sitting in your trades , wait until your PT gets hit and sell. And ofc always respect your stop loss! For a small account have a HARD stop . If your stop loss is 10% as soon as you see that hit get out the trade. This will help you long term because your wins will be bigger.

author: JABARI
content:
Giving yourself 6 months to come to a conclusion like this isn’t realistic. Trading takes time, just like starting any other business or going to university to get a degree. It’s not a get rich quick scheme. I’m not sure what expectations you had coming into trading, but even trading 1-3 times a week already puts you at a bit of a disadvantage when you’re day trading. You need to be attending every trading day to build that momentum.

As for the trades they take, the announce it as soon as they take it, but options move very quickly and put your entry at a disadvantage especially when you’re waiting for someone else to give you a signal through a livestream. On top of that your broker matters as well with how they’re filling your order. in regards to the candles, there are different entry criteria’s they’ve went through extensively and it’s the same one they follow, the difference is they’re way more experienced with candle formations, discretion, and their edge that they will enter sooner than you can think to enter. 
You can always compare your entry time to the livestream they upload and see if it lines up, and I can assure you it will line up. 

Another thing about tony making 25k+ is because of his position size and the way he scales those cons - you’re not able to do that with your account size and psychology at this time unfortunately. It’s better you continue practising with 3 cons and always leave the last one as a runner so you can build experience or you can always paper trade using more cons to practise scaling.

Stay consistent, give yourself more room and time and be optimistic.

author: Dee

The best thing you can do is to try and gain conviction for yourself, so you can have some accountability. It sucks when you follow their trade and you lose, vs when you follow your own idea and you lose money. Give yourself the opportunity to learn from your mistakes and figure out your own style

author: 305 Trader
content: I wanted to give you a heads up on 0DTE. I spent probsbly an hour with Charles Schwab on getting some explanation from the Greeks on my loss. The underlying QQQ moved 50 cts adversely but my loss was 40 cts in that same range.  This means an 80% loss in value to $1 of the underlying value in just 5 mins trade. Charles Schwab says that market makers do not like to explain the reason why DELTA is "undefined" and "non-relevant" their words on 0DTE. They encouraged me to use 1 DTE if I could, becuase DELTA then is "more relevant".  thats how far their help went. So, the lesson is, if trading 0DTE, be very picky with the Stop Loss while it is kept real. Because in my case it was just 50 cts Stop loss but the outcome was unexpectedly at .80 DELTA. Also, the IV goes higher as you further away from the ATM, keep that in mind too. I was on the 3rd slot at OTM.

author: Dan Grand
content:
Hey everyone, I wanted to share something important that I realized about my trading journey, and I’m hoping to get some guidance and support from the community.

Back in August, I had an amazing month trading options — one of my best ever. That gave me the confidence to try trading with real money. My first week was +$800, the second week I lost -$1000, and that’s when I realized I didn’t truly understand risk the way I thought I did. I stepped back and tried futures for a while, but trading futures alone without a mentor has been difficult. Watching YouTube isn’t enough when you don’t have someone to ask real questions.

And now, after thinking deeply, I’ve decided not to give up on options. I want to continue trading options seriously, because this is the environment where I feel the most support and structure, and where I’ve already seen my best results. I just want to do it the right way this time.

One big realization I had is this:

I can follow rules.
I can take clean entries.
I can respect my stop.
I can let my trades hit either TP or SL.

But what I cannot do well yet is read the higher-timeframe context and understand when the market wants to move long and when it wants to move short. That’s the piece I’ve never deeply worked on.

Sometimes I take a valid entry according to my rules, but I’m trading it completely against the larger direction — while some of you or the mentors are actually looking for trades in the opposite direction because of context. This made me realize I’m not just “pressing buttons.” I’m missing a whole dimension of the market.

So as I return to trading options, I really want to improve this part of my trading.
If anyone can share advice or help me build a simple, structured plan for learning how to read higher-timeframe context, it would mean a lot.

Thanks in advance for any support or suggestions. I appreciate this community and I’m ready to grow properly this time.

author: Hayden
content:
Sure 👍 

Truth is, numbers alone don’t show the full story. They don’t show the bad trades I passed on, the discipline it took to sit on my hands, or the weeks where protecting capital mattered more than making it. Mostly important is the reasons why 

P&L can create the wrong focus, invite comparison, and push people into forcing trades just to “keep up"

But I also believe in transparency. Everyday I do coaching and talk about my setups / PNL / scaling and why I didn't take trades. If you really want something that's going to help, those sessions and thoughts will go a lot further than numbers will 😅

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
I want to add some perspective here.
I’ve personally witnessed Neto grow into a highly consistent trader over the past year+. He didn’t just “show up” one day — he put in the work, learned from his mistakes, and earned his role as a coach/moderator through contribution and consistency. He has openly acknowledged his trade wins and his trade struggles over time, from small trades to larger ones.
Regarding P&L:
Sharing exact profit numbers is personal financial information. No trader — moderator or not — is obligated to disclose that. Some days we share contract size or P&L, some days we don’t. I do the same. In fact, I intentionally stopped sharing profit screenshots once trades exceed ~$2–3k because it can negatively impact newer traders’ psychology and motivation.
Cutting off P&L does not equal deception. It often equals:
protecting personal financial privacy
preventing unhealthy comparison
keeping focus on execution, process, and structure — not dollar amounts
This community is about education, discipline, and consistency, not flexing numbers or fueling speculation.

author: Huy
content:
just based on the way you ask these questions I can tell you are feeling really down..probably at the lowest point. I was there when I joined 2 months ago so I know exactly how it is. Here's my recommendation;
- take a few days off and just listen in. Ask Jack and Tony questions in the chat during the live trade if you have any. Stay calm; you would be very surprised on what you can see
- paper trade and or set the long / short position tool and pretend-trade to see how you do
- if you must take a live trade then go with just 1 contract for now
    - if you understand greeks and how it affects the premium; perhaps avoid OTM but rather go deeper ITM. This is because options are less sensitive to the underlying. A .7 / .8 delta will move more like a stock (just make sure the deep ITM options is liquid and the spread isn't too wide)

get a few wins with 1 contract play then you'll watch your confidence sky rocket

content: yeah we want to avoid elevated ivs..thats why you hear Jack kept saying we want to avoid trading a ticker before it has earnings since the IV is extremely high. But overall, it also depends on your risk tolerance. If you like slow moving tickers and are only interested in slow 20-30 cents move; you can look at other tickers like WMT; TGT; PG etc

author: Luke
content: Hey <@1402284752110882909>, once the market opens the day after earnings, the IV is going to be very high on the options contracts. Say we were looking to go long on TSLA the day after earnings, in order for us to go long we need to see some big red candles because those candles will crush the "call options'" IV, making it go way down. When you play names with very high IV the R:R never makes sense because usually you're risking a lot more money to make a smaller ammount. Hope that helps

author: Luke
content: If you have a strict ruleset that you're currently trying to follow, one thing that I've done in the past is I belive it's called "corrective discipline." If you break a single rule in a morning, you have to force yourself to instantly go do something that you don't want to do. You're basically punishing yourself for breaking your rules. If you go a period of time without breaking your rules, then you can have a "reward" or "mini celebration" planned. That's just something that really helped me in the past. Because then if you're about to break a rule based on emotions, your brain will naturally warn you to step back since it knows it's going to have to do that thing you don't want to do. Hope that helps.

author: Lianne
content:
There was a talk yesterday about how not to break your rules and <@1062934985109160057> gave an excellent idea. ‘Corrective discipline’. Everytime you break a rule, you have to do something you don’t want to do. To have my rules perfectly clear, I made a card with my rules that I will keep with me every session. It’s in dutch, but it says: 
- trade with the trend
- don’t sell if there’s no reason 
- only A+ setups 
- stick to your plan 
- choose max 5 tickers 
- lost 2 times? Stop 
- pay attention to ticker trend, don’t go against it 
- trade patterns, not P&L
- wait for confirmation

I could think of more rules, but I know if the list is too long, I will get lost and forget some. 

My ‘punishment’ is working out. As a competition Latin/ballroom dancer I also should do strength training, but I hate it 😅 so, this month I will get better in trading according my rules, or I will be in much better shape on the dancefloor lol 

Thanks for the advice Luke! 🙏🏽

author: Hugo Comte
content:
Hello,

Could someone share their Stats for Reclaim entry to the downside ?

After crunching data:

Reclaim entry PUTS: 4/4 looser. 0% win rate basically 
Reclaim entry CALLS: 1/9 looser: 88% win rate 

This is on the period since October ( as my trading was too poor before to generate useful data ) 

As per my data ( yes the sample size is small but still, its pretty contrasting ) , the reclaim entry doesn't work on the downside. We saw it very well on friday with TSLA.
If we had waited for a break and retest, there were no entry > no loss.

Curious to hear if someone can confirm with their data ( or show the contrary )

<@717554586843611217> <@801169196015943731> You have the largest sample size here, it would be helpfull to get your data in order to confirm or rule out this entry to the downside.

author: Hana
content:
Yesterday I lost $3K — a little over half my account. MU and ASTS dropped hard and my contracts were down about 60%.

The trade felt valid… until it wasn’t.

What’s messing with me is that hours later the move actually came. That’s the part that hits mentally more than the loss itself.

Now my account is at $1,600 and I’m honestly asking:

How are we supposed to react in moments like this?

Do you just accept it and move on?
Do you laugh it off?
Do you sit with it and reflect?
Or do you regret not holding and wonder “what if”?

Because holding to -80% hoping for a reversal… that’s not easy either. Most people would’ve been wiped before seeing that move.

I know I sized bigger than I should have — and I’ll own that. But I’m really curious how you all handle this mentally when it happens.

author: Angel (az)
content: Sure thing. Yes they teach candle close for entry. So in reality, they trade differently and are able to enter/exit with more discretion because of their many years of experience whereas we need to build an edge, which requires keeping things simple and the same every day so we can repeat it. The most repeatable unit of time is candle closure. You could say 20s to candle close, as long as you stick with that every single trade, that’s fine too. I would say it’s ok whatever you end up choosing with your stop logic but you must pick one and then stick with it, and not let it become true discretion. I see it as we have not yet earned the right to discretion. It’ll come in time, after we prove consistency!

author: 305 Trader
content: Angel, hi. What do you trade? 0DTE or >1DTE? This is the first consideration. Theres is a huge difference in the way to trade. And this is something is not being taught. In order to make it as a 0DTE trader, you have to lose small and exit as soon as the trade is not taking off. Then, observe the PA and if its still in your favor, consider reenter. Also, you need abundant confirmation, which is what <@1325884177291280475> does and teaches. Also <@801169196015943731> and he calls it a “conservative” approach in his videos. What Tony teaches to exit in the closed candle and that “tails do the damage” is in my view as a 0DTE trader only, only to be implemented when you are trading >1DTE. Why? In 0DTE there is bo intrinsic value of the contracts, their value is only extrinsic, which means, they go up (or down) only because of volatility and time decay. The more Out of the money you are, is even worst for options, that is also another consideration. Options are not straightforward. Suggest you become familiar with IV% and its actual pattern during the trading day. Enter only when IV is rising for longs and puts. But this requires, very tight really tight stop losses for 0DTEs.

Today is FRIDAY.

author: TonyMontana
content:
I lost $30k trying to learn trading.

It was my full networth 5 years ago.

Blessed to be able to call $30k just another week now.

As a new trader start with SMALL size, LEARN the skill (your in the right community), and most importantly embrace the losses as lessons.

It can be hard seeing others win when you’re losing.

It can also be hard remaining grounded when you’re winning.

In this room our message is clear:

We are NOT a signal service but we are an educational community. 

We want traders to come in and SEE how we view the markets, not to just enter because we are.

With this being said, these last couple months we have seen fantastic results and I’m glad you are able to see both the WINS & LOSSES in real time.

Hope yall had a good week.

I’ll catch you in the trade review tonight.

You guys see the good & bad.

author: TonyMontana
content:
**$75,000 Week Reflection**

I've never done this but since I didn't take a trade today I wanted to try something new.

When I started trading it was solely for money.

Yes, people will say it's for a noble cause, family, helping people, etc...

No.

We all start trading to make money, this is the reality.

The noble causes we use the money for come after, don't get that confused because I've been able to help many people **after seeing consistency**

However, in this chase we often feel behind.

Some of you may be making $10k/month while looking at my P&L and think "I NEED to do more"

Some of you I've spoken to personally making $50k/month and you think your behind.

Others of you may have just joined and still unprofitable.

At the end of the day, I know we are all here to make money and grow not only as traders but people because trading will truly expose your real self.

With this being said, let's try something new tomorrow.

Let's go into the day (all of us) without P&L on screen.

Remove your profit and loss screen from trading.

So all we see is the entry (if trade sets up) and exit.

We simply trade the chart, no emotions involved. 

If we win, fantastic

If we lose, fantastic

I think if 90% of people add this small tweak in their trading it would help tremendously.

It will show you a new perspective of trading the CHART with no bias.

If you like it, you can do it next week as well.

See y'all tomorrow.

author: Jdub
content:
Biggest Day Of The Year For Me!!

+12K Realized Intraday Plus Some Trailers Still
+17K Unrealized In Long Term 🙂

Beautiful trade today on TSLA. Hopefully some of you guys were able to capture it. I try my very best to outline the moves before hand in the pre market and in the live sessions. The reason I do them is for you guys to build conviction yourself. There is no better feeling then having your own conviction in a trade. That's why we always preach understanding whats going on instead of relying on "signals". You can follow signals all day but in the end you won't become profitable if don't learn to trade for yourself.

With that being said I just took some time to reflect today and I wanted to let you guys know that trading is hard. We might make it seem easier then it is at times but the reality is that trading is one of the most difficult professions in the world. 

I started trading in 2019. At the time I was living with my grandparents in an unfinished basement. I was in school, was working at Amazon while trying to make trading work. For the first two years I was losing every-single day and it felt like I couldn't even put on a winning trade. Nothing felt like it was going my way. I was struggling everyday but I knew that it was only a matter of time. 

I know some of you guys are in that position right now and I just want to let you guys know that it is going to get better. Everything that you need to succeed in the markets is all in front of you. All you have to do is put in the work. Show up to the pre markets preps, watch the live sessions over and over again, watch the trade reviews. We can layout the full blue print but in the end it is up to YOU to put in the work. I can't come and click the buttons for you. 

Just wanted to end it off here. I usually don't get too emotional or like typing out these motivational speeches but I thought if it can help someone or motivate someone then it would be worth it.  At the end of the day trading is very lonel

author: Jdub
content:
I've said it once before but I will say it again. 

These market have plenty of liquidity and volatility. What occurs in this case is moves happen quicker. 
The higher timeframes is not as clear and that's why you have so many intraday reversals. 

What happens in this case is you have to size down and have a wider stop if you want to risk the same amount as before.

Other things to note is don't marry a position. Things can move very quickly to either side. That's why I'm currently not putting on any swings since you can't hold onto a position for more than 2 hours. 

Also it makes it harder to manage positions with this volatility so I will refrain from calling out positions later into the day unless the trade is very clear. 

Make sure to actively monitor your positions if you have any. With that being said I will see you guys tm morning for CPI.

content:
Breakeven week for me 

Full transparency very tough past two weeks in the markets ever since the trump tariffs news

Trades setups will look good but ultimately not work out just due to the context of the overall markets.

The key is to not only protect your capital its also to protect your mental capital.

author: Jdub
content:
My biggest trade of the year

I want to take a moment to talk about what the Accelerator really means to me.

When I started this, I didn’t know exactly what it would turn into. I just knew I wanted to build a place where real trading happens. No hindsight, no hiding, no cherry picking. Just live trading, real execution, real wins, real losses, and real growth.

This was the first time I was going to live trade in front of so many people on a consistent basis and I put a lot of pressure on myself to perform and to continuously trade well day in and day out as many traders were watching every step along the way. 

After almost 2 years of live trading and showing up LIVE every single day through premarket prep, live trading, and weekly trade reviews. I can honestly say this has been my best trading year ever. Not just in P&L, but in execution, discipline, and psychology. And even better, I’ve watched so many of you transform as traders right alongside me.

The growth of this community has been unreal. The Accelerator is what it is today because of you. This is easily one of the strongest, most real trading communities in the industry and we’re only getting started.

With that being said we're going to do something special for the next 24 Hours.

Stay tuned. @everyone

author: MambaTrades
content:
What’s up everyone! 👋

Super excited to be here! I’ve been trading for 5 years and went all-in on futures last year.

I’m based in Southern California, and my trading name is inspired by my childhood hero, Kobe Bryant—RIP to the GOAT! 🐍

What I love about futures is the ability to manage risk effectively without battling theta decay. My system uses a 1-candle stop loss, allowing for precision and tight risk control.

If you're a newer trader or working with limited capital, futures are the best way to go. They allow you to develop a strong system and gain real experience without high costs. Once things start clicking, you can leverage prop firms and scale to $10K+ per month.

I primarily trade the 4 major indices (ES, NQ, RTY, YM) but focus mostly on ES and NQ because they offer consistent moves and high-quality risk-reward setups almost daily.

My approach is level-to-level trading based on liquidity and market structure. I’m here to share my system and processes to help you build an edge, develop strong trading habits, and master your psychology. 

Big shoutout to Jack and Tony for the opportunity—let’s crush it together! 🫡 

@here

author: MambaTrades
content:
One of my biggest issues the past 2-3 weeks of trading was being married to my daily bias and not being adaptive to change in price action and volume. 

For example, we've had a lot of gap downs and gap fills up and I kept trying to short the move up because I was married with the higher timeframe bias/narrative. Not good for this current market conditions with all the news, tariffs, and dip buyers. 

Moral of the story is be flexible and be willing to adapt your bias if price tells you a different story. 

Have a great trading day @here

content:
low probability conditions today since we expanded 294 points on NQ before market open which lead to huge consolidation. Very tough price action Monday and Tuesday so make sure to reflect and take notes for the next time we have a pending rate decision @everyone

Monday - We expanded in the premarket then ranged
Tuesday - we expanded in the premarket then ranged.

There's a pattern there that can save you a lot of losses and ultimately get you out of the break-even phase. 

Profitability is being able to identify poor market conditions and sit out then execute on the high probability trend days with size.

author: MambaTrades
content:
Reminder that this is not normal price action. We expanded 3406 points (15% on NQ) in 11 trading days. NQ will sometimes move that much in 6-12 months. The same move down took over 2 months of price actions. 

Don't get too overconfident and start sizing up because the market will humble you. Protect your capital and profits especially if you're up the past 2 weeks. After a huge distribution move, the market will accumulate and range eventually.

Remain humble, follow your plan daily, and don't get too cocky  because that will lead to overtrading and oversizing when the market starts to slow down/range. For now, ride the trend @everyone

author: MambaTrades

17% move on the Nasdaq in 13 trading days. Absolutely insane @everyone

I was definitely wrong in thinking we had to have a pullback in this move. This kind of move hasnt happened since 2020 and definitely a learning experience. Follow the trend and the market can always go up higher or lower than you expect it to. 

The market will now factor in interest rates lowering, lack of war fears, oil prices, lower inflation. Earnings coming up so another catalyst that can push us past ATH

author: MambaTrades
content: A psychology trick I use: Every day I come into the trading day "expecting to take 0 trades." If the setup appears, then I take it. This makes me more patient because I am expecting nothing from the market and only reacting based on my plan. Try this out, its a game changer @everyone
